problem_summary: 2025 MCM/ICM Problem D (operations_research / urban transportation network optimization). Team #2519935 modeled Baltimore's road/transit system as a multi-layer Arc-Node network and addressed three sub-tasks: (1) assess the impact of the 2024 Francis Scott Key Bridge collapse on commuters vs. non-commuters and on surrounding regions; (2) recommend optimal placement of new bus routes to improve the bus system; (3) propose a single transit project (a new "Orange Line" subway) that best improves residents' lives and analyze its effects on stakeholders and existing transit. The work was built on provided OSM-style edge/node data plus AADT/AAWDT traffic-count files, with heavy data preprocessing.
models_used: Multi-layer Arc-Node network model: graph G=(N,A) decomposed into layer subgraphs G_i by road class (motorway/primary/secondary/residential), with per-arc attributes d(u,v) shortest length, L(u,v) layer, t(u,v)=d/v(L) travel time, and flow-conservation constraint sum q(u,v)=sum q(v,u)

Impact Assessment (IA) model for Task 1: uses AADT (Annual Avg Daily Traffic) and AAWDT (Annual Avg Weekday Traffic) to algebraically separate traffic into commuters J and non-commuters K via a 5-weekday/7-day decomposition (AADT=(5J+7K)/7, AAWDT=(5J+5K)/5 -> solve for J,K); defines per-road impact coefficient beta_i = J_current/J_2024 (and analogous for K), with beta>1 = increase, beta<1 = decrease post-collapse
Historical-average baseline: estimates 2024 pre-collapse AADT/AAWDT per road as the 9-year mean (2014-2022) to form a counterfactual

Closeness Centrality on a 39-node highway subnetwork: C_C(v)=(N-1)/sum_t d(v,t), compared between graph G2 (bridge intact, node 37-39 edge present) and G (bridge collapsed, edge removed) to measure regional connectivity loss
Three-layer Bus Network (TBN) model for Task 2: classifies bus stops into Core / Bridge / Outer layers

Improved weighted k-shell decomposition: weighted degree k_s=[k_i^alpha (sum w_ij)^beta]^(1/(alpha+beta)); enriched with a station attribute score S_i = w_p*POI_i + w_r*N_i + w_k*k_i (POI types weighted: railway station 1.5, subway 1.0, universities 0.75, tourist attraction 0.7, markets 0.5), reduced to k_s=sqrt(k_i*S); k_s discretized into 0.05 bins; layer boundaries found via a breakpoint in high k_s (core/bridge) and the peak of cumulative-k_s (bridge/outer)
Entropy-weight + TOPSIS method to objectively weight the indicators (POI vs. passenger volume N_i) feeding the station score S

Improved (hierarchical) Dijkstra for Task 3: shortest path on time-weighted multi-layer graph, objective min T = sum t(i,j) = sum a(u,v)/v(L(u,v)), used to compute time-to-downtown / time-to-airport per station

Double Density Fitting (DDF) model for Task 3c: grids Baltimore (6x6, refined to 20x20) and regresses bus-stop density on road-network density; best fit is a quadratic y=16241x^2 - 15507x + 4751; subway impact measured as residual Delta rho = rho_actual - rho_predicted along existing subway corridors, extrapolated to the proposed line
key_techniques: Algebraic stakeholder disaggregation: turning two aggregate traffic indicators (AADT, AAWDT) into separate commuter/non-commuter volumes via a weekday/weekend linear system — a clever way to extract hidden structure from provided data instead of assuming it
Counterfactual via historical mean: using 2014-2022 averages as the 'bridge-still-standing' baseline so the collapse impact is a before/after delta rather than an absolute number
Graph-edit + recomputed centrality as a clean impact metric: deleting the single bridge edge and re-running closeness centrality isolates the structural effect on regional connectivity
Domain-enriched node importance: extending vanilla k-shell with POI semantics and passenger counts so 'importance' reflects real urban function, not just topology
Entropy-weight-TOPSIS to defend weight choices objectively (and they even document the rationale in the AI-use appendix)
Data-driven layer cutoffs: locating the core/bridge boundary at a k_s breakpoint and the bridge/outer boundary at the peak of cumulative k_s, rather than picking thresholds arbitrarily

Residual-based causal inference: fitting an expected bus-stop density from road density, then attributing the positive residual along subway corridors to the subway's presence (a difference-from-expected design)
Grid-resolution sensitivity analysis: re-running the DDF impact from 2400 m down to 120 m cells to show the qualitative conclusion (subway boosts adjacent bus demand) is robust

Serious data preprocessing with documented rules: imputing 354,420 missing road names via osmid uniqueness, removing 9,953 multi-osmid outliers, a slope-comparison 'road smoothing' rule |k3-k1|<|k3-k2| to merge ambiguous segments, and abbreviation normalization (ST->STREET) to join the AADT file to the edge file
why_it_won: "From a judge's view, this paper wins on three fronts. First, real data discipline: rather than hand-waving the messy provided dataset, the team documents concrete, reproducible cleaning rules (354,420 imputations, 9,953 outliers removed, a geometric road-smoothing test, abbreviation join keys) — this signals trustworthiness before any model runs. Second, every task uses a task-appropriate, named OR/network method with a clear quantitative payoff: the AADT/AAWDT algebra produces a defensible per-stakeholder impact coefficient; closeness-centrality-on-edge-deletion gives a crisp regional-impact story (node 39 most affected, +14% commuter flow on the Harbor Tunnel Thruway, -25% non-commuter); the enriched k-shell + entropy-TOPSIS yields an actionable layered bus design with headline numbers (+18.3% core-bridge edges, -82.9% outer-layer); and the DDF residual model converts a vague 'subway helps buses' claim into a measured ~2.31x demand factor. Third, it closes the loop the prompt actually asks for: explicit per-stakeholder analysis (commuters, tourists, students, flight passengers, business owners), a concrete recommended project with real station names and a 65-minute farthest-station-to-airport figure, a safety argument (grade-separated rail), sensitivity analysis, and a mayor-addressed memorandum. It reads as a coherent decision document, not just a modeling exercise."
section_highlights: "Summary leads with hard numbers per task (+14%/-25% flows, +18.3%/-82.9% edge composition, 65 min to airport, 2.31x bus demand) so a judge sees results in 30 seconds. Section 1.3 'Our Work' is a one-page visual pipeline (problems->foundations->Task1/2/3->sensitivity->safety) that orients the reader. The data preprocessing section (3.1-3.4) is unusually concrete and is itself a selling point. Each task follows a consistent model->solution->results rhythm. Strong figure storytelling: the top/bottom-100 affected-road map (Fig 11) physically shows traffic rerouting onto 'Bridge-II' and the Beltway; the three-layer bus maps (Fig 13) and before/after road-condition bar chart (Fig 16) make the optimization tangible; the time-to-airport line chart (Fig 20) directly answers the stakeholder question. The memorandum (Section 10) reframes both proposals in plain language for the mayor and honestly names downsides (operating cost, long construction, downtown disruption). The AI-use appendix transparently logs three ChatGPT queries (subway speed, Baltimore transit context, TOPSIS-entropy rationale)."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The AADT/AAWDT-to-commuter algebra rests on a rigid assumption (commuters travel exactly 5 weekdays; non-commuter volume identical on weekdays and weekends), and the 'current' post-collapse AADT/AAWDT values are not clearly sourced — the impact coefficients (e.g. beta up to 11.994) ride entirely on that decomposition and a 9-year-average counterfactual that assumes near-constant growth
Impact analysis is highway/main-road-centric; the team itself flags it misses local roads and non-car modes (cycling, walking, ride-share)

TBN layer boundaries (core/bridge/outer breakpoints, the 0.05 k_s bin, alpha=beta=1, the POI weight table) are partly hand-chosen / subjective — acknowledged as a weakness; entropy-TOPSIS objectifies only the two top-level indicator weights, not the layer cuts

The DDF quadratic (16241x^2 - 15507x + 4751) is fit on coarse grid aggregates with few points and is extrapolated from two existing subway corridors to a brand-new line — a strong causal leap with no validation set; residual-as-causal-effect ignores confounders (density and transit co-locate for many reasons)

No real optimization is solved for the bus/subway routes — 'optimal' placement is heuristic (connect under-served layers, draw a 2 km radius around the 11 core stops) rather than the output of a formal objective/ILP; the min-time Dijkstra is used for evaluation, not for route design

Several figures/tables in the source are visibly garbled or placeholder (e.g. the 89-node abstraction graph, the difference heatmap), and some equations have typos (G=G1 ∪ G1 ∪ G2...), suggesting figure/result fidelity issues that the judges tolerated but a reproducer cannot fully trust

Many inputs (POI locations, on/off passenger counts N_i, the 'four proposed metro lines' it selects from) are pulled from outside literature/assumptions without rigorous geocoding to the provided network
reusable_patterns: Recover hidden sub-populations from aggregate indicators by writing a small linear system over time buckets (the AADT/AAWDT -> commuter/non-commuter trick generalizes to any weekday/weekend or peak/off-peak split)
Build a counterfactual baseline from multi-year historical means, then express 'impact' as a ratio coefficient (>1 up, <1 down) that is easy to map and rank
Measure the effect of removing/adding infrastructure by editing one edge in the graph and recomputing a centrality metric (closeness/betweenness) before vs. after
Enrich a purely topological importance metric (k-shell, k-core, degree) with domain attributes (POI weights, passenger counts) so node tiers reflect real-world function
Use entropy-weight + TOPSIS whenever you must combine multiple heterogeneous indicators into one score and want to pre-empt the 'where did your weights come from' question
Find category cutoffs from the data shape — a derivative/breakpoint in the sorted metric, and the peak of a cumulative sum — instead of arbitrary thresholds

Quantify a 'X boosts Y' claim with a residual model: regress Y on a baseline predictor, then attribute the positive residual along the treated region to the intervention; defend it with a grid-resolution sensitivity sweep

Lead the Summary with one headline number per task; mirror the prompt's stakeholder list explicitly (commuters, tourists, students, businesses, flight passengers) in the results so judges can check coverage; close with a decision-maker-addressed memo that names real places and honestly lists downsides
Make data preprocessing a first-class, documented section with exact counts and concrete rules — it builds judge trust before the modeling even starts
